expectations.text: Uses 'hire' and/or 'fire' language to describe how customers choose or abandon products
Names or clearly invokes the three dimensions of a job (functional, emotional, social)
expectations.evidence: Scorecard row 'Emotional + social dimensions understood? 2/10' and 'Treated purely as functional/transactional problem' together invoke all three dimensions (functional, emotional, social) as the JTBD framework.

expectations.text: Frames the job in terms of circumstances/context, not customer demographics or attributes
expectations.evidence: Analysis is framed entirely around timing and context ('Cancelling at month 2 is a Little Hire failure,' 'usage frequency drops sharply after week 2-3,' 'what was happening that week?') with no mention of customer demographics.

expectations.text: Reframes the problem as a job-mismatch or competing-solution problem, not a pricing/marketing problem
expectations.evidence: Opens with 'Lower pricing, onboarding emails, and a referral program all treat churn as an acquisition/pricing problem' then reframes: 'you're losing them to non-consumption, because the product never became a habit.' Three hypotheses are all job-mismatch framings.

expectations.text: Asks what users are switching TO (what is getting hired in place of this product)
expectations.evidence: Interview step includes 'What are you using instead now? (this reveals the real competitor, often non-consumption)' and Forces section names Pull as 'going back to spreadsheets, manual work, or just not doing the job anymore.'
